description:
You are acting as a network-operations engineer who has been asked to produce a very small, machine-readable “first-pass” connectivity report from the current Linux machine.  
    Follow the exact steps below, capturing the exit status of every command that you run.  
    1. Create the directory “/home/user/network_diagnostics”.  
    2. Inside that directory create the file “diagnostics.csv”.  
    3. Run the five (and only the five) commands listed below **in the exact order shown** and, immediately after each command finishes, append one CSV line to “/home/user/network_diagnostics/diagnostics.csv” using this precise format:  

       ISO8601-timestamp,full_command_string,exit_status  

       • The timestamp must be generated with:  
         date '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z'  
       • The full_command_string must appear exactly as written below (including spacing).  
       • exit_status is the numeric value in “$?” returned by the command you just ran.  
       • Do NOT add header rows, extra spaces, or additional columns. Each command therefore adds exactly one line, so the final file will contain five lines in total.  

       The five commands you must execute are:  
       1. ping -c 4 127.0.0.1  
       2. ping -c 4 192.0.2.1  
       3. getent hosts localhost  
       4. ip -4 addr show lo  
       5. ss -ltn  

       Example of a single, correctly formatted line (yours will have a different timestamp and possibly a different exit status):  
       2024-03-17T15:42:19+0000,ping -c 4 127.0.0.1,0  

    4. When finished, verify that:  
       • The file “/home/user/network_diagnostics/diagnostics.csv” exists.  
       • It contains exactly 5 non-empty lines.  
       • Each line has exactly two commas (therefore three fields).  
       • The commands appear in the file in the exact sequence given above.  

    Do not include any additional commentary in the CSV file—only the five data lines.  
    Once you are confident the file meets the specifications, you are done.
truth:
The grading script will look for the following:

    File: /home/user/network_diagnostics/diagnostics.csv
    Expected properties:
        – Exists and is readable.
        – Exactly 5 lines long.
        – Each line matches the regex:
          ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}[+-][0-9]{4},(ping -c 4 127\.0\.0\.1|ping -c 4 192\.0\.2\.1|getent hosts localhost|ip -4 addr show lo|ss -ltn),[0-9]+$
        – Lines appear in this exact order:
              1: ping -c 4 127.0.0.1   (exit status must be 0)
              2: ping -c 4 192.0.2.1   (exit status must be non-zero; we accept 1 or any other value >0)
              3: getent hosts localhost (exit status must be 0)
              4: ip -4 addr show lo    (exit status must be 0)
              5: ss -ltn               (exit status must be 0)
        – No extra blank lines or trailing whitespace.

    Directory “/home/user/network_diagnostics” will NOT exist before the task starts; the agent must create it with default user permissions (umask 022 typically).  The grader simply requires the directory to exist and contain the single correctly-formatted diagnostics.csv file.
name: task_000000_fa4143d8
